title: Doro Wat

overview.description: Ethiopia's famous chicken stew, slowly cooked with onions, berbere spice and hard-boiled eggs. Rich, deeply flavoured and wonderfully comforting, it's considered one of the country's national dishes.

method: Heat the vegetable oil in a large casserole over a low heat and add the onions. Cook gently, stirring regularly, for about 35–40 minutes until they become soft, sweet and lightly caramelised.
Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Stir in the berbere spice, paprika, ginger and tomato purée. Cook for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add the butter and stir until melted into the onions.
Pour in the chicken stock and mix well.
Add the chicken thighs, cover and simmer gently for 45 minutes until the chicken is tender.
Meanwhile, prick each hard-boiled egg several times with a skewer or fork so it can absorb the sauce.
Add the eggs during the final 10 minutes of cooking.
Finish with the lemon juice, season to taste and serve hot.

learning.did-you-know: Doro Wat is traditionally served during celebrations and holidays throughout Ethiopia. The rich sauce is built almost entirely from slowly cooked onions rather than cream or flour.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How slowly cooking onions creates incredible depth of flavour, how berbere spice differs from Indian curry powders, and why the eggs are added at the end.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: One of the most iconic African dishes and a brilliant introduction to Ethiopian cooking. It expands the planner into a cuisine that many people have never attempted at home.
chapman-tips: Don't rush the onions—they're the secret to an authentic Doro Wat.
Berbere spice blends vary considerably, so taste before adding extra.
If using bone-in chicken, you'll get an even richer sauce.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze the stew without the eggs for the best texture. Add freshly boiled eggs when reheating if possible.
notes.leftovers: The flavour improves overnight as the spices continue to develop.
